items.q: Does light always travel at the same speed?
items.correct: Only in a vacuum; in any material medium (water, glass) light travels more slowly, which is what causes refraction.
items.misconception: Yes — c is a universal constant in every situation.
items.random_wrong: No — light always slows with distance from its source.
items.why: Students take 'c is constant' as 'c everywhere' and miss the qualifier 'in a vacuum'.

items.q: For a projectile launched horizontally, what affects the horizontal component of velocity during flight (ignoring air resistance)?
items.correct: Nothing — horizontal velocity stays constant; only vertical velocity changes due to gravity.
items.misconception: Gravity slows down both the vertical and horizontal motion.
items.random_wrong: The horizontal velocity increases over time because the projectile is falling.
items.why: Students treat 'gravity affects all motion' too broadly, missing that gravity acts only vertically and the components are independent.

items.q: How does the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons depend on light intensity?
items.correct: It does not — max KE depends only on the frequency of the light; intensity affects the rate (number) of electrons emitted.
items.misconception: Higher intensity gives photoelectrons more kinetic energy.
items.random_wrong: Intensity has no effect on anything in the photoelectric effect.
items.why: Students confuse 'brighter light' with 'more energetic photons'; intensity gives more photons, but each one's energy is set by frequency.

items.q: A proton consists of which quarks?
items.why: Correct answer: uud. “udd” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In β⁻ decay, the exchange particle is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: W⁻ boson. “Photon” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
